item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah those are valid points, I kind of struggled with that part a lot when writing this. Thanks for the upvote and the feedback, I'll see if I can get to it when I have more time :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP and the Xia snake-people, so this definitely gets an up-vote. However, one point of criticism is that I have serious trouble envisioning what the described objects are supposed to look like from the description.
2481-1: Is the "flat surface" referenced the floor of the anomalous area? How are the cube and pillars arranged (like, does the cube rest on the pillars, do the pillars stick from the top, etc)? Is the current cubical shape a result of being severed (indicating that the structure was taller), or was the damage from the severing relatively minor?
2481-2: Does "cylindrical with one pointed end" mean 'Cone-shaped', 'truncated cylinder', 'Cylindrical with one end tapered into a point', or 'Cylinder except one of the bases is tear-shaped'?

item.title: Re: Tiny nitpick
item.content:encoded: On a more meta-level, I think that given the location of Site-143 (Henan, China), it makes sense that the transcriber would probably also be a local mainlander or educated as such, and thus be used to using at least some Simplified Chinese during the transcription process.

item.title: Re: Grammar stuff
item.content:encoded: The mistakes have been corrected. Thanks a lot :3

item.title: Grammar stuff
item.content:encoded:
Second time reading this, finally understood the gist of what was going on, and I really like it. However, I must reference a few grammar/spelling/typing errors that I've noticed:

Here they are
close

SCP-2481-1 is to be is to be kept disassembled in storage, when not studied or experimented on.

"is to be" is repeated

Unlike SCP-2481-1, SCP-2481-2 displays little evidence of heat damage, but appears to have been damaged by a blunt force

"by blunt force", without the "a"

SCP-2481-3 claims to be a scholar/official of the Xia Dynasty under the reign of King Jie3, who was tasked with eliminating a hostile entity

"an hostile entity"

Footnote 1: SCP-2481 was discovered accidentally during the archaeological dig of the said Shang Dynasty site. (…)

"dig of said Shang Dynasty site"

Footnote 3: Allegedly the last king of Xia Dynasty, commonly described at as tyrant.

"described as a tyrant"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
VERY nice SCP, and I don't really have anything to say, so I'll just +1 and …

Congrats on a well deserved +100!

item.title: Re: Tiny nitpick
item.content:encoded: Considering that SunnyClockwork is from Mainland China, Simplified Chinese would be her preference. I can rationalise this as the in-universe researchers knowing Simplified characters and using that as the final transcription. While 2481-3 is not likely to actually write in Simplified characters, it could be rendered from its native script.

item.title: Re: Tiny nitpick
item.content:encoded: My guess would be that it's intentional; SunnyClockwork's an admin over at SCP-CN. Could be as simple as that she likes that variant better; she's an artist and we can be funny like that.

item.title: Tiny nitpick
item.content:encoded:
Disclaimer: I am from Taiwan. I really mean no disrespect.

The Old Chinese was great. A little thing that bugs me while reading it is the fact that some of the words are Simplified Chinese version. It's most distracting in words like "dragon" and "spirit".

Was it intentional? Do you need any help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah I'm aware of that part of mythology, just can't incorporate it properly so I just used the part of her creating humans. (Come to think of it I should properly describe her in more neutral terms, as the snake people in the end, while respecting them to a degree, aslo deem both gods as dangerous. Will keep this in mind when I expand on this ._.)

And yeah the researcher is female.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seeing nüwa as a female dragon full of rage is kinda weird, because in the Chinese mythology she is the one who fixed the world after it was partially destroyed (pillar was broken or something). She chose rocks and melted them and stuff.
Also, the name Xue Qing sounds like a nice name for a girl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't sure about the snake people, or sneople, before, but I feel like I've grown to like them. I'm still kind of unsure about the inclusion of the Children of the Torch, and have trouble picturing the actual space that the anomaly's located in.

But overall, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I can't help to imagine an ancient chinese snake person, or snerson, firing a miniature Scranton Reality Anchor shaped like a retro space laser pistol at a radioactive 3-legged crow. Pew! Pew! Pew!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've just realised! -3 is Hou Yi!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanon'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tickles the history/mythology parts of my brain in all the right ways. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am deeply amused by the Japanese translation for that which google suggested. It also works in a weird way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the record, I am willing to petition for 射日 to be the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kill the suns. kill…what?
You finally did it, great job!

But 'kill sunny' sounds really interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's a wild theory: The Foundation eventually figured out how 2481-2 works and build their own version of it called Project Metatron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: MrWrong is totally right, this is Foundation's re-transcribing so yeah :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never got to read that skip so nope :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2481-2 sounds similar to Project Metatron. Was that intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am surprised you did not nitpick that it should be right-to-left, since most pre-modern Chinese text are written that way.

But this is technically the Foundation re-transcribing whatever -3 has written down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love skips that tie in the real myths. I'm a complete sucker for them. I do have one question, should the chinese script be oriented vertically? Or it doesn't matter since it is simplified?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While it is a dense article, it does serve as an interesting enough take on Chinese Myth to keep my attention. The idea that an entire dynasty was a massive anomaly is really fun, and it would be interesting to see that expanded upon, maybe in a tale or two.

+1 Sunny. Huzzah to your first skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this in the forums, and I love it here.

Have a +1. ^_^

item.title: Nicely done.
item.content:encoded:
Well done Sunny!

Although at first I was sceptic reading about another kind of superweapon, the last addendum and the documents won me over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If only my culture'ssss hisssstory was interessssting enough to Ssskippify..

+1 for Ssssnake People

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've done some tales but this would be the first attempt at an SCP article here…

Great thanks to Metaphysician for writing comments from Dr. Low in the supplement, helping with the grammar, phrasing and the skip in general. Special thanks to Anborough, whose "Children of the Torch" series inspired a major part of the skip. Aslo special thanks to Palewalker for suggestions on Chinese text.

Huge thanks to Athena_Grey, A_Random_Day, minmin, MrWrong, Jekeled, Lex1nat0r, faminepulse, EldritchCadence, Jazstar, Conwell, TwsitedGears, NinjaPhoenix, AidenEldritch, Rimple, Feline, Tuomey, JackIke, Roget, ztshp , shaggydredlocks, MrHat, Steeltitan, Magnobile, ChaoSera, Bunton and others that reviewed the draft.

If this turns out successful, I'll try do more Chinese snake people stuff :3

Credit:

+Show Credits
-Hide Credits

The line "日安不到? 烛龙何照?" is from the famous poem Heavenly Questions by Qu Yuan (around 300 BC).

The lines "采九州之金", "收九州之金", and "收天下之金" in Document-2481 is based on "收天下之兵" from The Faults of Qin by Jia Yi (200~169 BCE).

The line "天柱折，地维绝" in Document-2481 is from 《淮南子·天文》/ Huainanzi, Tianwen or Patterns of Heaven section (Han Dynasty).

The line "有兄弟八十一人，皆铜头铁额。" in Document-2481 is based on "黄帝摄政前，有蚩尤兄弟八十一人，并兽身人语，铜头铁额，食沙石子，造立兵杖、刀、戟、大弩，威振天下。" from 《龙鱼河图》 (Han Dynasty).

The line "帝断其首，复生口、目，再战。帝灼之，方死，葬于常羊之山。" in Document-2481 is based on "帝断其首，葬之常羊之山" from 《山海经·海外西经》/ Classic of Mountains and Seas, Classic of Regions Beyond the Seas: West (4th century BC, fully formed in early Han Dynasty).

Footnote 4, "A Taoist concept, consisting of eight trigrams, often used to represent fundamental principles of reality." is based on "The Bagua or Pa Kua are eight symbols used in Taoist cosmology to represent the fundamental principles of reality, seen as a range of eight interrelated concepts." from Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bagua

The Addendum and Document-2481 are generally inspired by ancient Chinese myths.
